original_hypothesis.title: TimelockTokenPool Withdrawal Signature Lacks Domain Separation - Cross-Chain Replay
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The withdraw(address,bytes) function recovers the recipient from a signature over keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)). This hash includes no chain ID, contract address, nonce, or expiry. A valid signature observed on-chain on one chain can be replayed on any other chain where the same recipient has a grant in another TimelockTokenPool instance.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The signed message is deterministic and context-free. If TimelockTokenPool is deployed on multiple chains (L1, L2, testnet) with the same recipient, a signature from one chain is valid on all others. Since transactions are publicly observable after inclusion, an attacker extracts _sig from a completed tx and replays it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)) has no chainid, no address(this), no nonce

preconditions: Same recipient address has grants in TimelockTokenPool on multiple chains
Recipient has executed withdraw(address,bytes) on at least one chain (signature observable on-chain)
Unlocked tokens available on target chain

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Signature extracted from completed transaction on chain A, replayed on chain B
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker forces recipient's tokens on chain B to be sent to _to address, which may be an address attacker controls on chain B but recipient controls on chain A

economic_questions: Are TimelockTokenPool instances deployed on multiple chains with overlapping recipients?
Can _to be a contract address controlled by attacker on the target chain?
What is the total value of grants exposed to cross-chain replay?
validation_requirements: Verify multi-chain deployment from deployment scripts
Construct cross-chain replay scenario with concrete _to address
Calculate maximum extractable value across all chains

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire unlocked grant amount on target chain

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after observing legitimate withdraw tx on source chain
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, direct token theft

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% of unlocked tokens on target chain

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if multiple TimelockTokenPool instances exist across chains
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify ECDSA.recover produces same address cross-chain with same signature

validation_result.impact_summary: The withdraw(address,bytes) function uses a signature without chain ID, contract address, nonce, or expiry. A valid signature can be replayed across any chain or contract instance where the same recipient has a grant, forcing token withdrawal to a potentially uncontrolled address.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The TimelockTokenPool.withdraw(address _to, bytes memory _sig) function recovers the signer from keccak256(abi.encodePacked("Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ", _to)). This hash is entirely deterministic and context-free — it binds only to the _to address. It lacks block.chainid, address(this), a nonce, and an expiry timestamp. This means any valid signature observed on-chain (from a completed transaction) can be replayed on any other chain where the same signer (recipient) has a grant in a different TimelockTokenPool instance. Same-contract replay is mitigated by the amountWithdrawn accounting (subsequent calls yield amountToWithdraw=0), but cross-chain and cross-contract replay are completely unprotected. The contract's own NatSpec comments state 'We should deploy multiple instances of this contract for different roles', confirming that multiple instances are expected.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: TimelockTokenPool.sol line 172 shows: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked("Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ", _to)); This hash contains no domain-binding elements. ECDSA.recover on line 173 will return the same address regardless of which chain or contract the signature is submitted to. The _withdraw function on line 197 then processes the withdrawal using the recovered recipient address, sending tokens to _to.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The signed hash includes only a static prefix string and the _to address. No chain ID (block.chainid), no contract address (address(this)), no nonce, and no expiry. A signature produced for one chain/contract is valid on all others.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker observes legitimate withdraw(address,bytes) transaction on chain A, extracts _to and _sig from calldata
2. Attacker calls withdraw(_to, _sig) on chain B's TimelockTokenPool where same recipient has a grant
3. Line 172: hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)) — identical hash on both chains
4. Line 173: ECDSA.recover(hash, _sig) returns the same recipient address on chain B
5. Line 174: _withdraw(recipient, _to) is called, transferring chain B's unlocked tokens to _to
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The signature verification at line 172-173 produces the same result regardless of execution context. The hash keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)) is deterministic given _to. ECDSA.recover returns the same address for the same (hash, sig) pair on any chain. _withdraw at line 197 then checks getMyGrantSummary for the recovered recipient, computes amountToWithdraw based on the chain B grant's vesting schedule, and transfers tokens from sharedVault to _to via IERC20(taikoToken).transferFrom(sharedVault, _to, amountToWithdraw) at line 206.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Multiple TimelockTokenPool instances may be deployed across chains or for different roles
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No TimelockTokenPool deployment script is provided in the deployment scripts. However, the contract's NatSpec at lines 17-21 explicitly states: 'We should deploy multiple instances of this contract for different roles: investors, team members, advisors, etc., grant program grantees'. This confirms the design intent for multiple deployments.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. Recipient Alice has grants on both L1 and L2 TimelockTokenPool instances. 2. Alice signs a withdrawal message for _to = 0xABC on L2, specifying where her L2 tokens should go. 3. Alice's withdraw(0xABC, sig) transaction executes on L2 — publicly visible. 4. Attacker extracts sig from the L2 transaction calldata. 5. Attacker calls withdraw(0xABC, sig) on L1 TimelockTokenPool. 6. hash is identical (same _to, same prefix string). 7. ECDSA.recover returns Alice's address. 8. _withdraw sends Alice's L1 unlocked tokens to 0xABC. If 0xABC is a contract Alice deployed on L2 but doesn't control on L1 (e.g., different CREATE2 deployer, or undeployed on L1), the tokens may be permanently lost. Even if Alice controls 0xABC on both chains, the attacker forces an unwanted withdrawal timing, and the costToken safeTransferFrom may revert if Alice hasn't approved costToken on L1, causing a grief/DoS scenario.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The signature scheme violates standard domain-separation best practices (EIP-712, SWC-121). If the same recipient has grants across multiple TimelockTokenPool instances (different chains or different role pools on the same chain), any signature for one instance is valid for all. This enables forced withdrawals to potentially uncontrolled addresses, token theft if _to is an address controlled differently across chains, and forced timing of withdrawals the recipient did not intend.
recommended_mitigations: Include block.chainid and address(this) in the signed hash: keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.chainid, address(this), "Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ", _to, nonce))
Adopt EIP-712 structured data signing with a proper domain separator containing contract name, version, chainId, and verifyingContract
Add a per-recipient nonce that increments on each withdrawal to prevent any form of replay
Add an expiry timestamp to the signed message so signatures have a limited validity window
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy two TimelockTokenPool instances (simulating two chains) with the same recipient having grants on both. 2. Generate a valid signature for withdraw(_to, sig) on instance A. 3. Call withdraw(_to, sig) on instance A — observe success. 4. Call withdraw(_to, sig) on instance B using the same _to and sig. 5. Verify that instance B's tokens are sent to _to, confirming cross-instance replay.
uncertainties: Whether TimelockTokenPool is actually deployed on multiple chains with overlapping recipients in production — deployment scripts don't show this but NatSpec suggests multiple instances
Whether recipients typically use EOA addresses for _to (which they'd control on all chains) or contract addresses (which might differ across chains)
Whether costPerToken is zero for grants, which affects whether the forced withdrawal causes the costToken.safeTransferFrom to revert
additional_testing_needed: Test cross-instance replay with concrete grant parameters
Test the scenario where costPerToken > 0 and recipient has not approved costToken on the target chain
